▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design a calendar application: users create events with a start and end time on a calendar, the system detects scheduling conflicts (overlaps), and recurring events (daily/weekly/monthly) expand into concrete occurrences within a query window. Multiple users can book the same calendar concurrently without double-booking.

Core Requirements:

Add Events: add_event(calendar_id, title, time_range) stores a one-off event and rejects overlaps with a typed error.
Recurrence Rules: DAILY / WEEKLY / MONTHLY with an interval and either an UNTIL date or a COUNT (not both) — one Strategy per frequency.
Conflict / Overlap Detection: no two CONFIRMED events on a calendar may overlap; half-open intervals [start, end) so back-to-back meetings are allowed.
Expand Recurring Events: list_events(window) and find_conflicts(range) expand recurring events into concrete instances inside the query window.
Availability: is_free(range) answers "can I book this?".
Safe Concurrent Booking: multiple threads booking the same calendar never double-book (per-calendar lock).

Additional Considerations:

Atomic recurring add: if any occurrence clashes, the whole series is rejected.
Thread-safety under concurrent adds to the same calendar.

Design Considerations:

Use Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles.
Identify key classes (e.g. CalendarService, Event, TimeRange, RecurrenceRule, EventInstance).
Model recurrence as a Strategy (one rule per frequency) with a shared expansion template (Template Method).
Discuss design patterns (Strategy, Template Method, Value Object, Information Expert).
Focus on extensibility for new frequencies and a clean per-calendar concurrency model.

# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the Calendar application.

These are the "nouns": the vocabulary (enums), the typed failures (exceptions),
the value objects that never change (`TimeRange`, `EventInstance`), and the one
mutable entity (`Event`). The interesting logic lives in two places only:
`TimeRange.overlaps` (the half-open interval check that powers conflict
detection) and the recurrence rules in `strategies.py`.

🎙️ "The smart move here is the half-open interval. An event covers [start, end) —
start included, end excluded — so a meeting that ends at 11:00 and one that starts
at 11:00 do NOT conflict. That single convention makes overlap one boolean line
and kills the classic off-by-one 'touching counts as overlapping' bug."
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from enum import Enum
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    # Imported for type hints only. Importing it at runtime would create a cycle
    # (strategies.py imports Event/TimeRange/EventInstance from this module), so
    # the annotation below is a string and is never evaluated at runtime.
    from strategies import RecurrenceRule


# ── VOCABULARY ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class Frequency(Enum):
    """How often a recurring event repeats. One concrete rule per value."""

    DAILY = "DAILY"
    WEEKLY = "WEEKLY"
    MONTHLY = "MONTHLY"


class EventStatus(Enum):
    """Lifecycle of an event. Only CONFIRMED events count for conflicts."""

    CONFIRMED = "CONFIRMED"
    CANCELLED = "CANCELLED"


# ── EXCEPTIONS (one base, one subclass per failure mode) ─────────────────────
class CalendarError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this system raises — callers catch one type."""


class InvalidTimeRangeError(CalendarError):
    """A TimeRange was built with start not strictly before end."""


class InvalidRecurrenceError(CalendarError):
    """A recurrence rule had interval < 1, or both UNTIL and COUNT set."""


class ConflictError(CalendarError):
    """An add was rejected because the slot overlaps a CONFIRMED event."""


class CalendarNotFoundError(CalendarError):
    """An operation referenced a calendar id that does not exist."""


class EventNotFoundError(CalendarError):
    """remove_event was given an event id that is not in the calendar."""


# ── TIME RANGE: the value object that owns overlap logic ─────────────────────
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TimeRange:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # A half-open interval [start, end). Frozen because a span never changes
    # after it is created — if you want a different time, you make a new one.
    # It owns `overlaps` because the interval is the one thing that has all the
    # information needed to answer "do these two clash?" (Information Expert).
    start: datetime
    end: datetime

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        # WHY: a zero-length or backwards span is meaningless and would make the
        #      overlap math lie. We reject it at construction so no bad range can
        #      ever exist anywhere in the system.
        if self.start >= self.end:
            raise InvalidTimeRangeError(
                f"start {self.start.isoformat()} must be before end {self.end.isoformat()}"
            )

    def overlaps(self, other: "TimeRange") -> bool:
        # WHY: two half-open intervals clash only if each starts before the other
        #      ends. Because end is EXCLUDED, [10,11) and [11,12) return False —
        #      back-to-back meetings are allowed, only true overlaps are caught.
        return self.start < other.end and other.start < self.end

    @property
    def duration(self) -> timedelta:
        return self.end - self.start


# ── EVENT INSTANCE: one concrete occurrence in time ──────────────────────────
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class EventInstance:
    """One concrete occurrence of an event at a specific time.

    A single event expands to exactly one instance; a recurring event expands to
    many. Frozen because an occurrence is a snapshot — the expansion produced it,
    nothing mutates it afterwards.

    🎙️ "Instances are what callers actually see in a day/week view. The stored
    Event is the recipe; the instance is the cooked meal for one specific slot."
    """

    event_id: str
    title: str
    time_range: TimeRange
    status: EventStatus


# ── EVENT: the stored entity (single or recurring) ───────────────────────────
@dataclass
class Event:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # What the calendar stores. `time_range` is the FIRST occurrence. If
    # `recurrence` is set, this event repeats per that rule; if it is None, the
    # event happens exactly once. The orchestrator never reads the rule's guts —
    # it just calls `recurrence.expand(...)`, so adding a new frequency never
    # touches the service.
    id: str
    title: str
    time_range: TimeRange
    status: EventStatus = EventStatus.CONFIRMED
    recurrence: "RecurrenceRule | None" = None

    @property
    def is_recurring(self) -> bool:
        return self.recurrence is not None

# Python 3.11+
"""Recurrence rules — the Strategy seam for "how does this event repeat?".

One concrete rule per frequency (DAILY / WEEKLY / MONTHLY). The base class owns
the shared expansion loop (a Template Method); each subclass only fills in the
ONE thing that differs — how to compute the n-th occurrence's start.

🎙️ "The orchestrator never asks 'is this daily or monthly?'. It just calls
expand(event, window) and gets back concrete occurrences. Adding YEARLY tomorrow
is one new class that defines a single method — zero changes to the calendar."
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from calendar import monthrange
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

from models import (
    Event,
    EventInstance,
    Frequency,
    InvalidRecurrenceError,
    TimeRange,
)


class RecurrenceRule(ABC):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The abstraction the calendar depends on (Dependency Inversion). It holds
    # the three knobs every frequency shares — how many steps apart (interval),
    # a stop date (until) OR a stop count (count) — and the ONE expansion loop.
    # Subclasses never re-implement the loop; they only say where occurrence #n
    # begins. That keeps the tricky window/until/count logic in exactly one place.
    frequency: Frequency

    def __init__(
        self,
        interval: int = 1,
        until: datetime | None = None,
        count: int | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        # WHY: validate at construction so an impossible rule can never exist.
        #      interval < 1 would loop forever; until+count together is ambiguous
        #      (two different stop conditions) so we reject it outright.
        if interval < 1:
            raise InvalidRecurrenceError("interval must be >= 1")
        if until is not None and count is not None:
            raise InvalidRecurrenceError("set UNTIL or COUNT, not both")
        self.interval = interval
        self.until = until
        self.count = count

    @abstractmethod
    def _nth_start(self, base_start: datetime, n: int) -> datetime:
        """Return the start of occurrence #n (n=0 is the first), from base_start."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    def expand(self, event: Event, window: TimeRange) -> list[EventInstance]:
        # ── THE TEMPLATE METHOD ──────────────────────────────────────────────
        # Walk occurrences 0,1,2,... and keep the ones that overlap `window`.
        # Three independent stop conditions, checked every step:
        #   1. COUNT reached            -> we've emitted enough occurrences
        #   2. start passed UNTIL       -> the rule itself has ended
        #   3. start passed window.end  -> nothing later can overlap (starts only
        #                                  ever move forward), so we can stop early
        duration = event.time_range.duration
        instances: list[EventInstance] = []
        n = 0

        while True:
            if self.count is not None and n >= self.count:
                break
            start = self._nth_start(event.time_range.start, n)
            if self.until is not None and start > self.until:
                break
            if start > window.end:
                break

            occurrence = TimeRange(start, start + duration)
            if occurrence.overlaps(window):
                instances.append(
                    EventInstance(event.id, event.title, occurrence, event.status)
                )
            n += 1

        return instances


class DailyRule(RecurrenceRule):
    """Repeats every `interval` days."""

    frequency = Frequency.DAILY

    def _nth_start(self, base_start: datetime, n: int) -> datetime:
        return base_start + timedelta(days=self.interval * n)


class WeeklyRule(RecurrenceRule):
    """Repeats every `interval` weeks, on the same weekday/time as the first."""

    frequency = Frequency.WEEKLY

    def _nth_start(self, base_start: datetime, n: int) -> datetime:
        return base_start + timedelta(weeks=self.interval * n)


class MonthlyRule(RecurrenceRule):
    """Repeats every `interval` months, on the same day-of-month and time.

    Day-overflow is clamped: a rule anchored on the 31st lands on the 30th/28th
    in months that are shorter, so the occurrence always exists.
    """

    frequency = Frequency.MONTHLY

    def _nth_start(self, base_start: datetime, n: int) -> datetime:
        # WHY: months are not a fixed number of days, so we cannot use timedelta.
        #      We add months on the calendar, then clamp the day to that month's
        #      last day (e.g. Jan 31 + 1 month -> Feb 28) so the date is real.
        month_index = base_start.month - 1 + self.interval * n
        year = base_start.year + month_index // 12
        month = month_index % 12 + 1
        last_day = monthrange(year, month)[1]
        day = min(base_start.day, last_day)
        return base_start.replace(year=year, month=month, day=day)

# Python 3.11+
"""CalendarService — the orchestrator — plus its thread-safe subclass.

The clean `CalendarService` is single-threaded-correct and has ZERO lock code:
its `_lock` hook does nothing. `ThreadSafeCalendarService` overrides that ONE
method with a per-calendar reentrant lock, so the whole concurrency story is a
~12-line subclass and the core design stays readable.

The orchestrator owns the truth: a registry of calendars, each a list of stored
events. Conflict detection and recurrence expansion are the only real logic, and
both reuse the same two private helpers so single and recurring events go through
exactly one overlap path.
"""

import itertools
import threading
from contextlib import contextmanager
from datetime import timedelta
from typing import Iterator

from models import (
    CalendarNotFoundError,
    ConflictError,
    Event,
    EventInstance,
    EventStatus,
    EventNotFoundError,
    TimeRange,
)
from strategies import RecurrenceRule


class CalendarService:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The public API. External code only ever calls these six methods. It holds
    # every calendar and enforces the one rule that matters: no two CONFIRMED
    # events on the same calendar may overlap. It never reaches inside a
    # recurrence rule — it asks the rule to expand itself.

    # Used only when a recurring event has no UNTIL/COUNT: we still need a
    # bounded window to check it for conflicts at add time. One year ahead is a
    # pragmatic horizon for an interview; production would make this configurable.
    _CONFLICT_HORIZON_DAYS = 365

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._calendars: dict[str, list[Event]] = {}
        self._ids = itertools.count(1)

    # ── the swappable lock hook (Template Method) ──
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, calendar_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: does nothing in the clean version — there is zero lock code in
        #      this whole file. The thread-safe subclass replaces just this one
        #      method with a real per-calendar lock.
        yield

    def _next_id(self) -> str:
        # itertools.count.__next__ is atomic under the GIL, so a shared counter
        # is safe even when two calendars are written at once.
        return f"evt-{next(self._ids)}"

    # ── public API ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def add_event(self, calendar_id: str, title: str, time_range: TimeRange) -> Event:
        """Add a one-off event. Rejects if it overlaps a CONFIRMED event."""
        with self._lock(calendar_id):
            calendar = self._calendars.setdefault(calendar_id, [])
            conflicts = self._find_conflicts_locked(calendar, time_range)
            if conflicts:
                raise ConflictError(
                    f"'{title}' overlaps {len(conflicts)} existing occurrence(s) "
                    f"in calendar '{calendar_id}'"
                )
            event = Event(id=self._next_id(), title=title, time_range=time_range)
            calendar.append(event)
            return event

    def add_recurring_event(
        self,
        calendar_id: str,
        title: str,
        time_range: TimeRange,
        rule: RecurrenceRule,
    ) -> Event:
        """Add a recurring event. Rejects if ANY occurrence (within the check
        window) overlaps an existing CONFIRMED occurrence — the series is
        all-or-nothing, never half-booked."""
        with self._lock(calendar_id):
            calendar = self._calendars.setdefault(calendar_id, [])
            event = Event(
                id=self._next_id(),
                title=title,
                time_range=time_range,
                recurrence=rule,
            )
            check_window = self._conflict_window(time_range, rule)
            for occurrence in self._expand_event(event, check_window):
                if self._find_conflicts_locked(calendar, occurrence.time_range):
                    raise ConflictError(
                        f"recurring '{title}' occurrence at "
                        f"{occurrence.time_range.start.isoformat()} overlaps an "
                        f"existing event in calendar '{calendar_id}'"
                    )
            calendar.append(event)
            return event

    def remove_event(self, calendar_id: str, event_id: str) -> None:
        """Delete an event by id. Raises if the calendar or event is unknown."""
        with self._lock(calendar_id):
            calendar = self._calendars.get(calendar_id)
            if calendar is None:
                raise CalendarNotFoundError(f"unknown calendar '{calendar_id}'")
            for index, event in enumerate(calendar):
                if event.id == event_id:
                    del calendar[index]
                    return
            raise EventNotFoundError(
                f"event '{event_id}' not found in calendar '{calendar_id}'"
            )

    def find_conflicts(self, calendar_id: str, time_range: TimeRange) -> list[EventInstance]:
        """Return every existing occurrence that overlaps `time_range`."""
        with self._lock(calendar_id):
            calendar = self._calendars.get(calendar_id, [])
            return self._find_conflicts_locked(calendar, time_range)

    def list_events(self, calendar_id: str, window: TimeRange) -> list[EventInstance]:
        """Expand every event into concrete occurrences inside `window`, sorted
        by start time. Single events yield 0 or 1; recurring events yield many."""
        with self._lock(calendar_id):
            calendar = self._calendars.get(calendar_id, [])
            occurrences: list[EventInstance] = []
            for event in calendar:
                occurrences.extend(self._expand_event(event, window))
            occurrences.sort(key=lambda occ: occ.time_range.start)
            return occurrences

    def is_free(self, calendar_id: str, time_range: TimeRange) -> bool:
        """True if nothing overlaps `time_range`. Reentrant: it re-takes the same
        calendar lock via find_conflicts — that is why the lock must be an RLock."""
        with self._lock(calendar_id):
            return len(self.find_conflicts(calendar_id, time_range)) == 0

    # ── private helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def _expand_event(self, event: Event, window: TimeRange) -> list[EventInstance]:
        # WHY: one path for both kinds of event. A cancelled event contributes
        #      nothing; a single event is 0-or-1 occurrence; a recurring event
        #      delegates to its rule. Conflict checks and listings all funnel
        #      through here, so they can never disagree about what exists.
        if event.status == EventStatus.CANCELLED:
            return []
        if event.recurrence is None:
            if event.time_range.overlaps(window):
                return [
                    EventInstance(event.id, event.title, event.time_range, event.status)
                ]
            return []
        return event.recurrence.expand(event, window)

    def _find_conflicts_locked(
        self, calendar: list[Event], time_range: TimeRange
    ) -> list[EventInstance]:
        # WHY: expand each stored event over just the candidate slot and collect
        #      the occurrences that truly overlap. We scan the whole calendar —
        #      fine for the hundreds-of-events scale; §5.5-C notes when to index.
        conflicts: list[EventInstance] = []
        for event in calendar:
            for occurrence in self._expand_event(event, time_range):
                if occurrence.time_range.overlaps(time_range):
                    conflicts.append(occurrence)
        return conflicts

    def _conflict_window(self, first: TimeRange, rule: RecurrenceRule) -> TimeRange:
        # WHY: a recurring add needs a bounded window to check. If the rule has an
        #      UNTIL we check up to it; otherwise we check one horizon ahead. A
        #      COUNT-bounded rule stops itself inside expand regardless of this.
        if rule.until is not None:
            end = rule.until + first.duration
        else:
            end = first.start + timedelta(days=self._CONFLICT_HORIZON_DAYS)
        return TimeRange(first.start, max(end, first.end))


class ThreadSafeCalendarService(CalendarService):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The ONLY difference from the clean service: a real lock per calendar. Two
    # agents booking different calendars never block each other; two agents
    # booking the SAME calendar serialize, so the check-then-insert in add_event
    # is atomic and double-booking is impossible.
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self._locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()  # guards creation of per-calendar locks

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, calendar_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
        # Step 1: lock the map briefly to fetch-or-create this calendar's lock.
        #         Without this, two threads creating the SAME key at once could
        #         each make a different lock and defeat the whole scheme.
        with self._map_lock:
            lock = self._locks.get(calendar_id)
            if lock is None:
                lock = threading.RLock()
                self._locks[calendar_id] = lock
        # Step 2: hold the per-calendar lock for the actual work. RLock is
        #         reentrant so is_free() can call find_conflicts() — same thread,
        #         same lock — without deadlocking on the second acquire.
        with lock:
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the Calendar application.

Run: `python3 main.py`. Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK".
The demo uses the clean single-threaded CalendarService; the thread-safe
subclass and its Barrier proof live in the test suite.
"""

from datetime import datetime, timedelta

from calendar_service import CalendarService
from models import ConflictError, TimeRange
from strategies import WeeklyRule


def at(day: int, hour: int, minute: int = 0) -> datetime:
    """Tiny helper: a datetime in a fixed demo week (June 2026)."""
    return datetime(2026, 6, day, hour, minute)


def main() -> None:
    svc = CalendarService()
    cal = "alice"

    # ── Case 1: a single event is added and stored ──
    standup = svc.add_event(cal, "Standup", TimeRange(at(1, 9), at(1, 9, 30)))
    print(f"Case 1: added '{standup.title}' ({standup.id}) at 09:00-09:30")
    assert standup.id == "evt-1"
    print("Case 1: a one-off event is created and registered -> OK")

    # ── Case 2: a back-to-back, non-overlapping event is allowed ──
    review = svc.add_event(cal, "Review", TimeRange(at(1, 9, 30), at(1, 10, 30)))
    print(f"Case 2: added '{review.title}' at 09:30-10:30 (touches Standup, no overlap)")
    assert svc.is_free(cal, TimeRange(at(1, 11), at(1, 12))) is True
    print("Case 2: half-open intervals let meetings touch end-to-start -> OK")

    # ── Case 3: an overlapping event is rejected with a typed error ──
    try:
        svc.add_event(cal, "Oops", TimeRange(at(1, 10), at(1, 11)))
        assert False, "overlap should have been rejected"
    except ConflictError as exc:
        print(f"Case 3: overlapping add rejected -> {exc}")
    print("Case 3: a clashing event is refused, the invariant holds -> OK")

    # ── Case 4: a weekly recurring event expands inside a query window ──
    yoga_rule = WeeklyRule(interval=1, count=4)
    svc.add_event("bob", "Lunch", TimeRange(at(1, 12), at(1, 13)))  # separate calendar
    yoga = svc.add_recurring_event("bob", "Yoga", TimeRange(at(2, 18), at(2, 19)), yoga_rule)
    window = TimeRange(at(1, 0), at(30, 0))
    occurrences = [o for o in svc.list_events("bob", window) if o.title == "Yoga"]
    dates = [o.time_range.start.strftime("%b-%d %H:%M") for o in occurrences]
    print(f"Case 4: weekly '{yoga.title}' (every {yoga_rule.frequency.value}, count=4) -> {dates}")
    assert len(occurrences) == 4
    assert (occurrences[1].time_range.start - occurrences[0].time_range.start) == timedelta(weeks=1)
    print("Case 4: a recurring rule expands to concrete instances in a window -> OK")

    # ── Case 5: find_conflicts reports exactly which occurrences clash ──
    clashes = svc.find_conflicts("bob", TimeRange(at(9, 18, 30), at(9, 19, 30)))
    print(f"Case 5: querying Jun-09 18:30-19:30 finds {len(clashes)} clash -> {clashes[0].title}")
    assert len(clashes) == 1 and clashes[0].title == "Yoga"
    print("Case 5: conflict lookup pinpoints the overlapping occurrence -> OK")

    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import pathlib
import sys

# Put the workspace root on sys.path so `from models import ...` works in tests.
sys.path.insert(0, str(pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent))

# Python 3.11+
"""pytest suite for the Calendar application.

Covers: half-open overlap edge cases (touching vs overlapping vs contained),
recurrence expansion correctness (interval / UNTIL / COUNT for daily, weekly,
monthly incl. month-end clamping), the public orchestrator API and its typed
errors, and the MANDATORY concurrency proof — N threads racing to book the same
slot via threading.Barrier, asserting NO double-booking slips through.
"""

import threading
from datetime import datetime

import pytest

from calendar_service import CalendarService, ThreadSafeCalendarService
from models import (
    ConflictError,
    CalendarNotFoundError,
    EventNotFoundError,
    EventStatus,
    InvalidRecurrenceError,
    InvalidTimeRangeError,
    TimeRange,
)
from strategies import DailyRule, MonthlyRule, WeeklyRule


def dt(year: int, month: int, day: int, hour: int, minute: int = 0) -> datetime:
    return datetime(year, month, day, hour, minute)


def tr(start: datetime, end: datetime) -> TimeRange:
    return TimeRange(start, end)


# ── TimeRange overlap edge cases ─────────────────────────────────────────────
def test_touching_ranges_do_not_overlap():
    a = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11))
    b = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 11), dt(2026, 6, 1, 12))  # starts exactly when a ends
    assert a.overlaps(b) is False
    assert b.overlaps(a) is False


def test_overlapping_ranges_detected():
    a = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11))
    b = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10, 30), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11, 30))
    assert a.overlaps(b) is True and b.overlaps(a) is True


def test_contained_range_overlaps():
    outer = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 9), dt(2026, 6, 1, 17))
    inner = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 12), dt(2026, 6, 1, 13))
    assert outer.overlaps(inner) is True and inner.overlaps(outer) is True


def test_backwards_range_rejected():
    with pytest.raises(InvalidTimeRangeError):
        tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 11), dt(2026, 6, 1, 10))


def test_zero_length_range_rejected():
    with pytest.raises(InvalidTimeRangeError):
        tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 10))


# ── recurrence expansion: interval / count / until ───────────────────────────
def _starts(instances):
    return [i.time_range.start for i in instances]


def test_daily_count_limits_occurrences():
    svc = CalendarService()
    rule = DailyRule(interval=1, count=3)
    svc.add_recurring_event("c", "Daily", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 9), dt(2026, 6, 1, 10)), rule)
    window = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 0), dt(2026, 6, 30, 0))
    out = svc.list_events("c", window)
    assert _starts(out) == [dt(2026, 6, 1, 9), dt(2026, 6, 2, 9), dt(2026, 6, 3, 9)]


def test_daily_interval_skips_days():
    svc = CalendarService()
    rule = DailyRule(interval=2, count=3)  # every other day
    svc.add_recurring_event("c", "Daily2", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 9), dt(2026, 6, 1, 10)), rule)
    out = svc.list_events("c", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 0), dt(2026, 6, 30, 0)))
    assert _starts(out) == [dt(2026, 6, 1, 9), dt(2026, 6, 3, 9), dt(2026, 6, 5, 9)]


def test_weekly_until_stops_at_boundary():
    svc = CalendarService()
    rule = WeeklyRule(interval=1, until=dt(2026, 6, 20, 0))
    svc.add_recurring_event("c", "Weekly", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 18), dt(2026, 6, 1, 19)), rule)
    out = svc.list_events("c", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 0), dt(2026, 7, 30, 0)))
    # Jun 1, Jun 8, Jun 15 are <= UNTIL; Jun 22 is past it and excluded.
    assert _starts(out) == [dt(2026, 6, 1, 18), dt(2026, 6, 8, 18), dt(2026, 6, 15, 18)]


def test_weekly_window_clips_occurrences():
    svc = CalendarService()
    rule = WeeklyRule(interval=1, count=10)
    svc.add_recurring_event("c", "Weekly", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 18), dt(2026, 6, 1, 19)), rule)
    # Query only a 2-week window: only the occurrences inside it come back.
    out = svc.list_events("c", tr(dt(2026, 6, 7, 0), dt(2026, 6, 21, 0)))
    assert _starts(out) == [dt(2026, 6, 8, 18), dt(2026, 6, 15, 18)]


def test_monthly_clamps_to_month_end():
    svc = CalendarService()
    rule = MonthlyRule(interval=1, count=3)  # anchored on Jan 31
    svc.add_recurring_event("c", "Monthly", tr(dt(2026, 1, 31, 9), dt(2026, 1, 31, 10)), rule)
    out = svc.list_events("c", tr(dt(2026, 1, 1, 0), dt(2026, 4, 30, 0)))
    # Feb has no 31st -> clamps to Feb 28; Mar keeps 31.
    assert _starts(out) == [dt(2026, 1, 31, 9), dt(2026, 2, 28, 9), dt(2026, 3, 31, 9)]


def test_invalid_recurrence_rejected():
    with pytest.raises(InvalidRecurrenceError):
        DailyRule(interval=0)
    with pytest.raises(InvalidRecurrenceError):
        WeeklyRule(until=dt(2026, 6, 1, 0), count=3)  # both stop conditions


# ── orchestrator API + typed errors ──────────────────────────────────────────
def test_add_event_returns_confirmed_event():
    svc = CalendarService()
    evt = svc.add_event("c", "Meeting", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11)))
    assert evt.status == EventStatus.CONFIRMED and evt.id.startswith("evt-")


def test_overlapping_add_raises_conflict():
    svc = CalendarService()
    svc.add_event("c", "A", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11)))
    with pytest.raises(ConflictError):
        svc.add_event("c", "B", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10, 30), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11, 30)))


def test_touching_add_allowed():
    svc = CalendarService()
    svc.add_event("c", "A", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11)))
    evt = svc.add_event("c", "B", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 11), dt(2026, 6, 1, 12)))
    assert evt.title == "B"


def test_recurring_conflict_rejects_whole_series():
    svc = CalendarService()
    svc.add_event("c", "Block", tr(dt(2026, 6, 15, 18), dt(2026, 6, 15, 19)))
    rule = WeeklyRule(interval=1, count=4)
    with pytest.raises(ConflictError):
        # Jun 1, 8, 15, 22 — the Jun 15 occurrence clashes with Block.
        svc.add_recurring_event("c", "Yoga", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 18), dt(2026, 6, 1, 19)), rule)
    # The series was rejected as a whole — nothing partially added.
    assert svc.list_events("c", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 0), dt(2026, 6, 30, 0)))[0].title == "Block"


def test_is_free_reflects_state():
    svc = CalendarService()
    slot = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11))
    assert svc.is_free("c", slot) is True
    svc.add_event("c", "A", slot)
    assert svc.is_free("c", slot) is False


def test_find_conflicts_returns_instances():
    svc = CalendarService()
    rule = DailyRule(interval=1, count=5)
    svc.add_recurring_event("c", "Daily", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 9), dt(2026, 6, 1, 10)), rule)
    hits = svc.find_conflicts("c", tr(dt(2026, 6, 3, 9, 30), dt(2026, 6, 3, 10, 30)))
    assert len(hits) == 1 and hits[0].time_range.start == dt(2026, 6, 3, 9)


def test_remove_event_deletes():
    svc = CalendarService()
    evt = svc.add_event("c", "A", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11)))
    svc.remove_event("c", evt.id)
    assert svc.is_free("c", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11))) is True


def test_remove_unknown_event_raises():
    svc = CalendarService()
    svc.add_event("c", "A", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11)))
    with pytest.raises(EventNotFoundError):
        svc.remove_event("c", "evt-999")


def test_remove_from_unknown_calendar_raises():
    svc = CalendarService()
    with pytest.raises(CalendarNotFoundError):
        svc.remove_event("ghost", "evt-1")


# ── CONCURRENCY PROOF: no double-booking under a Barrier race ─────────────────
def test_no_double_booking_same_slot():
    """N threads race to book the SAME slot on one calendar. Exactly ONE wins;
    the rest get ConflictError. The invariant — no two CONFIRMED events overlap —
    must hold, proven by the calendar holding exactly one event for that slot."""
    svc = ThreadSafeCalendarService()
    n = 24
    slot = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 10), dt(2026, 6, 1, 11))
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
    wins: list[str] = []
    losses: list[Exception] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def attempt(i: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()  # all N hit add_event at the same instant
        try:
            evt = svc.add_event("team", f"Booking-{i}", slot)
            with guard:
                wins.append(evt.id)
        except ConflictError as exc:
            with guard:
                losses.append(exc)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(i,)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert len(wins) == 1, f"expected exactly 1 winner, got {len(wins)}"
    assert len(set(wins)) == 1
    assert len(losses) == n - 1
    assert all(isinstance(e, ConflictError) for e in losses)
    # Ground truth from the stored state, not from our counters:
    booked = svc.list_events("team", slot)
    assert len(booked) == 1, f"calendar must hold exactly one event, holds {len(booked)}"
    assert booked[0].event_id == wins[0]


def test_distinct_slots_all_succeed_concurrently():
    """N threads booking N NON-overlapping hourly slots all succeed — the lock
    serializes correctly without rejecting legitimate, non-clashing bookings."""
    svc = ThreadSafeCalendarService()
    n = 12
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
    wins: list[str] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def attempt(hour: int) -> None:
        slot = tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, hour), dt(2026, 6, 1, hour + 1))
        barrier.wait()
        evt = svc.add_event("team", f"Slot-{hour}", slot)
        with guard:
            wins.append(evt.id)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(h,)) for h in range(8, 8 + n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert len(wins) == n
    assert len(set(wins)) == n  # every booking is a distinct, unique event
    day = svc.list_events("team", tr(dt(2026, 6, 1, 0), dt(2026, 6, 2, 0)))
    assert len(day) == n
    # The invariant holds: no two confirmed occurrences overlap.
    for earlier, later in zip(day, day[1:]):
        assert not earlier.time_range.overlaps(later.time_range)

content.text:
All tests pass. Here's the complete, interview-ready design.

> 🎙️ **Open with the roadmap:** "I'll build a calendar that stores events, detects time conflicts, and expands recurring events into concrete occurrences. The one smart move is the half-open interval `[start, end)` — it makes overlap a single boolean and recurrence a clean Strategy. I'll keep the core single-threaded and clean, flag the booking race proactively, and show the per-calendar lock fix at the end."

## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0–5 min | **Say §1–2 out loud** — restate "events + recurrence + conflict detection", propose scope cuts (no invites/timezones/notifications for v1). | 🗣️ SAY |
| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — six public methods: `add_event`, `add_recurring_event`, `remove_event`, `find_conflicts`, `list_events`, `is_free`. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — `CalendarService`, `Event`, `TimeRange`, `RecurrenceRule` + 3 concretes, `EventInstance`. Mention enums verbally. | ✍️ DRAW |
| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — Strategy (recurrence) + Template Method (expand loop & lock hook), then: "there's a check-then-act race in `add_event` — I'll fix it with a per-calendar lock in §9." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — the half-open interval insight; how `expand` walks occurrences with three stop conditions. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** — enums → `TimeRange.overlaps` → `RecurrenceRule` + one concrete → `CalendarService` skeleton → `add_event` → `add_recurring_event`. | ✍️ WRITE |
| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 4** — single add + weekly expansion. In a coding env, run `main.py`. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-calendar `RLock` via the no-op `_lock` hook; write the subclass only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
| 55–60 min | **Follow-ups** — timezones, all-day events, infinite recurrence, distributed booking. | ❓ IF ASKED |

**Two interview modes:**
- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output; demo the three state traces verbally.
- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Run `main.py` and `pytest -q` in §7; the cases are proved by the program.

**The one thing that makes interviewers flag you "strong hire":** right after the diagram, say *"I see a check-then-act race in `add_event` — two bookers both see the slot free and both insert."* Saying it before you're asked signals you've built real concurrent systems.

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)

We're building the engine behind a calendar app like Google Calendar — the part that stores events with a start and end, stops you from double-booking the same slot, and handles repeating events ("every Monday", "the 1st of each month") without storing a row per occurrence forever. The interesting bits are three: deciding whether two events clash, turning a repeat-rule into the actual dates it produces inside a window you're looking at, and letting several people book the same shared calendar at once without two events landing on top of each other. I'll treat this as the back-end service — no UI, no email invites.

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)

**You:** "I'll model an event as a half-open interval `[start, end)` — start included, end excluded — so a meeting ending at 11:00 and one starting at 11:00 do *not* conflict. That kills the off-by-one debate up front. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep, that's the standard convention."
*✓ locked: back-to-back meetings are allowed; only true overlaps are conflicts.*

**You:** "For recurrence I'll support DAILY / WEEKLY / MONTHLY, each with an `interval` (every 2 weeks) and a stop condition that's either an `UNTIL` date or a `COUNT` — not both. Good enough for v1?"
**Interviewer:** "Good. Both-at-once should be an error, agreed."
*✓ scoped: rule = frequency + interval + (until XOR count).*

**You:** "I'll assume one shared timezone — naive datetimes — and treat timezones/DST as out of scope for this round, since that's a whole sub-system. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Fine for now; flag how you'd extend it."
*if pushed: store everything in UTC and convert at the edges — noted for §9.*

**You:** "The conflict rule is: no two CONFIRMED events on the *same* calendar may overlap. Different calendars are independent. An overlapping `add` is rejected with a typed error — no auto-queue, no force. Reasonable?"
**Interviewer:** "Yes — reject with a clear error."
*✓ the invariant the whole design protects: per-calendar, no two confirmed events overlap.*

**You:** "For a recurring add, if *any* generated occurrence clashes, I reject the whole series rather than booking it half-way. All-or-nothing. Agreed?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed — partial series would be confusing."
*✓ recurring add is atomic: it either all goes in or none of it does.*

**You:** "Last one — multiple people can book the same calendar concurrently, and I must guarantee no double-booking. I'll keep the core clean and single-threaded, then add a per-calendar lock. That's the concurrency story?"
**Interviewer:** "Exactly what I want to see."
*✓ this is the headline non-functional requirement — proven with a Barrier test in §9.*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**

1. **Add a one-off event** — `add_event(calendar_id, title, time_range)`
   - Stores the event if its slot is free; returns the created `Event`.
   - Rejects with `ConflictError` if it overlaps any CONFIRMED occurrence.
2. **Add a recurring event** — `add_recurring_event(calendar_id, title, time_range, rule)`
   - Expands the rule (DAILY/WEEKLY/MONTHLY, interval, until|count) and stores it if no occurrence clashes.
   - Rejects the *entire series* with `ConflictError` if any occurrence overlaps.
3. **Detect conflicts** — `find_conflicts(calendar_id, time_range)`
   - Returns every existing occurrence (single or expanded recurring) that overlaps the given range.
4. **List a window** — `list_events(calendar_id, window)`
   - Expands all events into concrete `EventInstance`s inside `window`, sorted by start.
5. **Check availability** — `is_free(calendar_id, time_range)`
   - True iff nothing overlaps the range.
6. **Remove an event** — `remove_event(calendar_id, event_id)`
   - Deletes by id; raises `EventNotFoundError` / `CalendarNotFoundError` for unknown ids.
7. **Safe concurrent booking** — multiple threads booking the same calendar never double-book; conflicting bookers get a typed error.

**Out of scope**
- Timezones / DST (single naive timezone assumed — separate sub-system).
- Invites, attendees, RSVPs, notifications (downstream — we just store and detect).
- Persistence / DB (in-memory registry; §9 covers the distributed version).
- All-day events, exceptions to a series ("skip this one Monday") — noted as twists in §9.

> 💬 **What you say to open:** "Before I model anything I want to lock down the conflict convention — half-open intervals, per-calendar, reject on overlap. With that, let me walk through the actors and flows."

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**
- **Organizer / user** — adds one-off and recurring events, removes events.
- **Viewer** — queries a window (`list_events`) or asks "am I free?" (`is_free`).
- **Concurrent bookers** — multiple threads/agents booking the same shared calendar at once (the concurrency driver).

**Core flows**
1. Organizer: `add_event` → service checks the slot is free → stores → returns the event (or rejects).
2. Organizer: `add_recurring_event` → service expands the rule in a check window → if every occurrence is clear, stores the series → returns the event.
3. Viewer: `list_events(window)` → service expands every stored event into concrete occurrences inside the window → sorted list back.
4. Viewer: `is_free(range)` / `find_conflicts(range)` → service expands existing events over that range → boolean / list of clashes.

## 3. Core Entities (accept / reject)

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough

> **Event** — A stored thing with a title, a time, a status, and maybe a repeat rule. It has identity and is referenced by occurrences. *Accepted — the central entity.*
>
> **TimeRange** — A start and an end. Tempting to leave as two loose datetimes on the Event, but the *overlap* logic has to live somewhere, and the range is the only thing that holds both ends. Giving it a class lets it own `overlaps` (Information Expert). *Accepted as a value object.*
>
> **RecurrenceRule** — "Every 2 weeks until July." This is pure behavior — given a base time and a window, produce occurrences. Different frequencies compute differently, so it's the perfect Strategy. *Accepted as an interface with one concrete per frequency.*
>
> **EventInstance** — One concrete occurrence at a specific time. A single event makes one; a recurring event makes many. Callers see *instances* in a day view, not the stored recipe. *Accepted as a value object — distinct from the stored Event.*
>
> **Calendar** — A named container of events with its own conflict boundary and its own lock. In v1 it has no behavior beyond "hold a list of events," so I model it as a *keyed list inside the orchestrator* rather than its own class — the orchestrator is the thing that enforces the no-overlap rule across that list. *Rejected as a class — it's a `calendar_id → list[Event]` entry; I'd promote it to a class the moment it grows owner/sharing/permission state.*
>
> **Frequency** — A fixed set DAILY/WEEKLY/MONTHLY with no behavior of its own. *Rejected as entity — an enum; the behavior lives in the rule subclasses.*
>
> **EventStatus** — CONFIRMED / CANCELLED. Finite vocabulary. *Rejected as entity — an enum; only CONFIRMED events count for conflicts.*
>
> **Organizer / Viewer** — Actors who call the API; the system doesn't store them in v1. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.*

### 3.2 Final entity table

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| `CalendarService` | Orchestrate all operations; enforce no-overlap per calendar | `_calendars: dict[str, list[Event]]`, `_ids: count` | No two CONFIRMED occurrences on one calendar overlap |
| `Event` | Hold one stored event (single or recurring) | `id: str`, `title: str`, `time_range: TimeRange` (first occurrence), `status: EventStatus`, `recurrence: RecurrenceRule \| None` | A recurring event delegates expansion to its rule; it never expands itself |
| `TimeRange` | A half-open interval and its overlap test | `start: datetime`, `end: datetime` | `start < end` always (rejected at construction) |
| `EventInstance` | One concrete occurrence produced by expansion | `event_id: str`, `title: str`, `time_range: TimeRange`, `status: EventStatus` | Immutable snapshot — frozen after creation |
| `RecurrenceRule` (abstract) | Expand a base event into occurrences in a window | `interval: int`, `until: datetime \| None`, `count: int \| None` | `interval >= 1`; `until` XOR `count` (never both) |

**Enums**
- `Frequency` = `DAILY`, `WEEKLY`, `MONTHLY`
- `EventStatus` = `CONFIRMED`, `CANCELLED`

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro

> "OK so the model has five objects. `CalendarService` is the brain — it owns the `calendar_id → events` map and is the only thing that enforces 'no two confirmed events overlap'. `Event` is what we store; it knows its first time slot and, if it repeats, *holds* a recurrence rule but never expands itself. `TimeRange` is the interesting little value object — it owns `overlaps`, because the interval is the only thing with both ends in hand. `RecurrenceRule` is the behavior — one subclass per frequency — and it produces `EventInstance`s, the concrete occurrences a day-view actually shows. The things I deliberately left OUT are `Calendar` — it's just a keyed list until it grows permissions — and `Frequency`/`Status`, which are enums, not entities."

## 3.5 API / System Interface

| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `add_event` | `(calendar_id: str, title: str, time_range: TimeRange) -> Event` | the stored event | `ConflictError`, `InvalidTimeRangeError` | Organizer books a one-off slot |
| `add_recurring_event` | `(calendar_id: str, title: str, time_range: TimeRange, rule: RecurrenceRule) -> Event` | the stored recurring event | `ConflictError`, `InvalidRecurrenceError`, `InvalidTimeRangeError` | Organizer books a repeating series (all-or-nothing) |
| `remove_event` | `(calendar_id: str, event_id: str) -> None` | — | `CalendarNotFoundError`, `EventNotFoundError` | Organizer deletes an event by id |
| `find_conflicts` | `(calendar_id: str, time_range: TimeRange) -> list[EventInstance]` | overlapping occurrences | — | Show exactly what clashes with a slot |
| `list_events` | `(calendar_id: str, window: TimeRange) -> list[EventInstance]` | occurrences in window, sorted | — | Render a day/week/month view |
| `is_free` | `(calendar_id: str, time_range: TimeRange) -> bool` | availability | — | Quick "can I book this?" check |

> 🎙️ "The whole system is six calls — two that take bookings in (`add_event`, `add_recurring_event`), three read queries (`find_conflicts`, `list_events`, `is_free`), and one delete. Notice there's no `expand` method on the surface — expansion is internal; callers think in occurrences, not rules. If you handed me this table cold, you'd know exactly what a calendar back-end does."

## 4. Class Design

### 4.1 Per-class derivation

#### CalendarService (the orchestrator) — first

**(a) Role** — the system's public API; every operation flows through it, and it's the single place that guarantees no two confirmed events overlap on a calendar.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What it must remember |
|---|---|
| "events live on a calendar; calendars are independent" | a map `calendar_id → list[Event]` |
| "ids are stable and unique" | a monotonic id counter |

```
class CalendarService:
    - _calendars: Map<string, Event[]>
    - _ids: counter
```

*State placement trade-off:* the per-calendar **lock** is relational state (it's about coordinating access to a calendar, not a property of any event), so it lives in the thread-safe orchestrator subclass, not on `Event`. An event's `status` is physical state — it lives on the event.

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method |
|---|---|
| "add a one-off event, reject overlaps" | `add_event(calendar_id, title, time_range) -> Event` |
| "add a recurring event, reject whole series on clash" | `add_recurring_event(calendar_id, title, time_range, rule) -> Event` |
| "delete by id" | `remove_event(calendar_id, event_id) -> None` |
| "what clashes with this slot?" | `find_conflicts(calendar_id, time_range) -> EventInstance[]` |
| "render a window" | `list_events(calendar_id, window) -> EventInstance[]` |
| "am I free?" | `is_free(calendar_id, time_range) -> bool` |

```
class CalendarService:
    - _calendars: Map<string, Event[]>
    - _ids: counter

    + CalendarService()
    + add_event(calendar_id, title, time_range) -> Event | ConflictError
    + add_recurring_event(calendar_id, title, time_range, rule) -> Event | ConflictError
    + remove_event(calendar_id, event_id) -> void | EventNotFoundError
    + find_conflicts(calendar_id, time_range) -> EventInstance[]
    + list_events(calendar_id, window) -> EventInstance[]
    + is_free(calendar_id, time_range) -> bool
    # _expand_event(event, window) -> EventInstance[]
    # _find_conflicts_locked(calendar, time_range) -> EventInstance[]
    # _conflict_window(first, rule) -> TimeRange
    # _lock(calendar_id)            // no-op hook; subclass overrides
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why one map of lists, not a `Calendar` class?** A calendar has no behavior in v1 beyond holding events; the no-overlap rule is cross-event and belongs to the orchestrator. Promote to a class when it gains owners/sharing.
- **Why is `_expand_event` private and there's no public `expand`?** Callers think in occurrences. Expansion is an implementation detail shared by `find_conflicts` and `list_events`, so it funnels through one private helper — they can never disagree about what exists.
- **Why a `_lock` hook that does nothing?** So the clean core has zero lock code; the thread-safe version overrides one method (Template Method). The diff is ~12 lines.

#### TimeRange (value object)

**(a) Role** — a half-open interval `[start, end)` that owns the overlap test.

**(b) State**

| Requirement | What it must remember |
|---|---|
| "events have a start and end" | `start`, `end` datetimes |

```
class TimeRange (frozen):
    - start: datetime
    - end: datetime
```

**(c) Operations**

| Need | Method |
|---|---|
| "do two slots clash?" | `overlaps(other) -> bool` |
| "how long is it?" (for cloning occurrences) | `duration -> timedelta` |

```
class TimeRange (frozen):
    - start: datetime
    - end: datetime

    + TimeRange(start, end)        // raises InvalidTimeRangeError if start >= end
    + overlaps(other) -> bool      // start < other.end AND other.start < end
    + duration -> timedelta
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why frozen?** A span never changes; if you want a different time you make a new one. Frozen makes it safe to share and hash.
- **Why does `overlaps` use strict `<`?** Because `end` is excluded — `[10,11)` and `[11,12)` return `False`, so touching meetings are allowed. That single choice removes the most common conflict-detection bug.

#### Event (entity)

**(a) Role** — the stored event; holds its first slot and, optionally, the rule that repeats it.

**(b) State**

| Requirement | What it must remember |
|---|---|
| "events have a title and identity" | `id`, `title` |
| "events have a time" | `time_range` (the FIRST occurrence) |
| "events can be cancelled" | `status` |
| "events may repeat" | `recurrence: RecurrenceRule \| None` |

```
class Event:
    - id: string
    - title: string
    - time_range: TimeRange
    - status: EventStatus
    - recurrence: RecurrenceRule | None
```

**(c) Operations**

| Need | Method |
|---|---|
| "is this a series or a one-off?" | `is_recurring -> bool` |

```
class Event:
    - id: string
    - title: string
    - time_range: TimeRange
    - status: EventStatus = CONFIRMED
    - recurrence: RecurrenceRule | None = None

    + is_recurring -> bool
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why does `Event` hold the rule instead of the service?** A recurring event *is* its rule + first slot; keeping them together means the service can expand any event uniformly via `event.recurrence.expand(...)` without a side-table to keep in sync.
- **Why is `time_range` the FIRST occurrence, not a list of all of them?** Storing every occurrence is unbounded (think "daily forever"). The rule is a compact generator; we materialize occurrences only inside a query window.

#### EventInstance (value object)

**(a) Role** — one concrete occurrence produced by expansion; what a day-view renders.

**(b) State**

| Requirement | What it must remember |
|---|---|
| "which event produced it" | `event_id`, `title` |
| "exactly when it happens" | `time_range` |
| "is it live" | `status` |

```
class EventInstance (frozen):
    - event_id: string
    - title: string
    - time_range: TimeRange
    - status: EventStatus
```

**(c) Operations** — none; it's an immutable snapshot read by callers.

| Need | Method |
|---|---|
| (none — pure data carrier) | — |

```
class EventInstance (frozen):
    - event_id: string
    - title: string
    - time_range: TimeRange
    - status: EventStatus
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why a separate type from `Event`?** One stored `Event` can yield many occurrences; an instance carries the *specific* slot. Mixing them would force callers to ask "is this the recipe or one cooked occurrence?"
- **Why frozen and carrying `event_id`?** So a caller can map an occurrence back to the stored event (e.g. to delete the series) without the instance being mutable.

#### RecurrenceRule (Strategy interface) + concretes

**(a) Role** — the seam for "how does this event repeat?". The base owns the expansion loop; each subclass supplies the one differing step — where occurrence #n begins.

**(b) State**

| Requirement | What it must remember |
|---|---|
| "every N days/weeks/months" | `interval` |
| "stop on a date" | `until` |
| "stop after N times" | `count` |

```
abstract class RecurrenceRule:
    - interval: int
    - until: datetime | None
    - count: int | None
    - frequency: Frequency
```

**(c) Operations**

| Need | Method |
|---|---|
| "give me the occurrences inside this window" | `expand(event, window) -> EventInstance[]` (template) |
| "where does occurrence #n start?" | `_nth_start(base_start, n) -> datetime` (abstract) |

```
abstract class RecurrenceRule:
    - interval: int
    - until: datetime | None
    - count: int | None
    - frequency: Frequency

    + RecurrenceRule(interval, until, count)   // validates interval>=1, until XOR count
    + expand(event, window) -> EventInstance[] // shared template method
    + _nth_start(base_start, n) -> datetime     // abstract — subclass fills in

class DailyRule(RecurrenceRule):
    + _nth_start(base_start, n) -> base_start + days(interval * n)

class WeeklyRule(RecurrenceRule):
    + _nth_start(base_start, n) -> base_start + weeks(interval * n)

class MonthlyRule(RecurrenceRule):
    + _nth_start(base_start, n) -> add (interval * n) months, clamp day to month-end
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why a Template Method for `expand`?** The hard part — three stop conditions (count, until, past-window) and window-clipping — is identical for every frequency. Only the date step differs, so only `_nth_start` is abstract. A new frequency is one tiny method, no risk of re-implementing the loop wrong.
- **Why `until` XOR `count`?** Two stop conditions at once is ambiguous; we reject it at construction so a contradictory rule can never exist.
- **Why does `MonthlyRule` clamp the day?** Because Feb has no 31st — a rule anchored on the 31st lands on Feb 28, so every occurrence is a real date.

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert

I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to its data. `TimeRange` owns `overlaps` because it's the only thing holding both ends of both intervals. `RecurrenceRule` owns expansion because the rule is the only thing that knows the repeat semantics. `CalendarService` owns conflict enforcement and the registry because it's the only thing that can see *all* events on a calendar at once — the no-overlap rule is cross-event, so it can't live on any single event.

### 5.2 Applied patterns

**Strategy — `RecurrenceRule`**
- **Why it fits HERE:** "how an event repeats" is exactly the axis the product will keep extending — daily/weekly/monthly today, weekday-only or "last Friday" tomorrow. Extracting `RecurrenceRule` means the service calls `rule.expand(event, window)` and never branches on frequency.
- **Interface + concrete:** `RecurrenceRule.expand(...)` → `DailyRule` / `WeeklyRule` / `MonthlyRule`.
- **🎙️ Script:** "The calendar doesn't decide how an event repeats — it asks the rule. Adding a new repeat pattern is a new class, not an `if` in the service."
- **💬 Layman gloss:** "It's like the calendar saying 'you tell me your repeat dates' instead of the calendar memorizing every kind of repeat itself."

**Template Method — `RecurrenceRule.expand` and `CalendarService._lock`**
- **Why it fits HERE:** the expansion *loop* (three stop conditions + window-clip) is identical across frequencies; only the date step differs — so the base owns the loop and subclasses fill `_nth_start`. Same shape for concurrency: the clean `_lock` is a no-op placeholder; the thread-safe subclass overrides just that method.
- **Interface + concrete:** `RecurrenceRule.expand` (final loop) → `_nth_start` (abstract hook); `CalendarService._lock` (no-op) → `ThreadSafeCalendarService._lock` (real RLock).
- **🎙️ Script:** "The tricky part is written once; the part that varies is a tiny overridable hook."
- **💬 Layman gloss:** "Same recipe every time — only one ingredient swaps out."

### 5.3 SOLID

- **Single Responsibility:** `TimeRange` owns overlap math, `RecurrenceRule` owns expansion, `CalendarService` owns coordination + the no-overlap invariant. Three reasons to change, three classes.
- **Open/Closed:** a new frequency is a new `RecurrenceRule` subclass — zero edits to the service.
- **Dependency Inversion:** `CalendarService` and `Event` depend on the `RecurrenceRule` abstraction, never on `WeeklyRule` directly.
- **Liskov:** any `RecurrenceRule` subclass is substitutable in `expand` — the base loop treats them identically.

### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS

Every claim above survives `Cmd-F`: `RecurrenceRule` is a real ABC with three real subclasses the service consumes through the abstraction (Strategy + DIP). `expand` is a concrete base method calling the abstract `_nth_start` (Template Method). `_lock` is a real no-op context manager overridden in `ThreadSafeCalendarService` (Template Method). I did **not** claim Factory (no enum→object translation in v1 — callers construct rules directly) or Observer (no notifications in scope). If we added a rule registry keyed by an RRULE string, *that* would earn Factory — but it's not wired in v1, so I won't name-drop it.

## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach

### A. The Key Insight

> **Insight:** Model every slot as a half-open interval `[start, end)`, and make recurrence a Strategy whose base class owns one expansion loop. Conflict detection becomes a single boolean; recurrence becomes "walk occurrences until a stop condition."
>
> **Why it works:** Overlap of half-open intervals is exactly `a.start < b.end AND b.start < a.end` — symmetric, no special cases, and back-to-back slots (`end == start`) correctly *don't* clash. Recurrence is monotonic: occurrence starts only move forward, so once a start passes the window's end you can stop. Both facts collapse messy logic into a few lines.
>
> **What breaks without it:** The naive version uses closed intervals (`<=`) and inlines status writes. With `<=`, a 10–11 meeting blocks an 11–12 one — users complain they "can't book back-to-back." And without the monotonic stop, an unbounded "daily forever" rule loops infinitely when you list a window. By making `TimeRange` own a half-open `overlaps` and `expand` stop at `window.end`, both bugs are impossible by construction.

### B. Per-Operation Algorithm

**`TimeRange.overlaps(other)`**
1. return `self.start < other.end AND other.start < self.end`

**`RecurrenceRule.expand(event, window)` — the generator (template)**
1. `duration = event.time_range.duration`; `n = 0`; `instances = []`
2. loop:
3.   if `count` set and `n >= count`: stop
4.   `start = _nth_start(base_start, n)`  ← the only frequency-specific step
5.   if `until` set and `start > until`: stop
6.   if `start > window.end`: stop  ← monotonic early-exit
7.   `occ = TimeRange(start, start + duration)`
8.   if `occ.overlaps(window)`: append `EventInstance(...)`
9.   `n += 1`
10. return `instances`

> 🎙️ "This is the generator — three stop conditions. Step 4 is the *only* line a subclass changes. Step 6 is the non-obvious one: because occurrence starts only move forward, the first time a start passes the window's end I can quit — that's what makes 'daily forever, show me one week' terminate."

**`add_event(calendar_id, title, time_range)` — the pipeline**
1. acquire `_lock(calendar_id)`  ← no-op in clean core; real lock in §9
2. `calendar = calendars.setdefault(calendar_id, [])`
3. `conflicts = _find_conflicts_locked(calendar, time_range)`
4. if `conflicts`: raise `ConflictError`
5. `event = Event(next_id(), title, time_range)`
6. `calendar.append(event)`
7. return `event`

> 🎙️ "This is the pipeline — check then insert. Steps 3–6 are the critical section: the conflict check and the append must be one atomic step, or two bookers both pass step 3. That's the race I flagged — §9 wraps 3–6 in a per-calendar lock."

**`add_recurring_event(...)` — the all-or-nothing pipeline**
1. acquire `_lock(calendar_id)`
2. `event = Event(next_id(), title, time_range, recurrence=rule)`
3. `window = _conflict_window(time_range, rule)`
4. for each `occ` in `_expand_event(event, window)`:
5.   if `_find_conflicts_locked(calendar, occ.time_range)`: raise `ConflictError`  ← reject whole series
6. `calendar.append(event)`; return `event`

**`find_conflicts(calendar_id, time_range)`**
1. acquire `_lock`; `calendar = calendars.get(calendar_id, [])`
2. return `_find_conflicts_locked(calendar, time_range)` — expand each event over the slot, keep overlapping occurrences

**`list_events(calendar_id, window)`**
1. acquire `_lock`; expand every event over `window`; sort by start; return

**`is_free(calendar_id, time_range)`**
1. acquire `_lock`; return `find_conflicts(...) == []`  ← re-enters the same lock (why RLock)

**`remove_event(calendar_id, event_id)`**
1. acquire `_lock`; if calendar unknown → `CalendarNotFoundError`
2. find by id and delete; if absent → `EventNotFoundError`

**(3) DRY RUN — `add_recurring_event("bob", "Yoga", [Jun-02 18:00, 19:00), WeeklyRule(count=4))` with `Block` already at Jun-15 18:00:**

| Step | Action | State change |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | build Event(recurrence=WeeklyRule) | `evt-2`, recurrence set |
| 3 | `_conflict_window` (count-bounded) | window `[Jun-02 18:00, Jun-02+365d)` |
| 4 | expand → occurrences | Jun-02, Jun-09, Jun-16, Jun-23 @ 18:00 (count=4) |
| 5a | check Jun-02 18:00–19:00 | no clash ✓ |
| 5b | check Jun-09 18:00–19:00 | no clash ✓ |
| 5c | check Jun-16 18:00–19:00 | no clash ✓ |
| 5d | check Jun-23 18:00–19:00 | no clash ✓ |
| 6 | append, return | calendar `bob` now holds `[Block, Yoga]` |

> *Edge probe: had `Block` been at Jun-16 instead, step 5c raises `ConflictError` and nothing is appended — the series is rejected as a whole, never half-booked.*

### C. Data Structure Choices

| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative) | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `dict[calendar_id → list[Event]]` | the registry | O(1) calendar lookup; calendars are independent so a flat map beats one global list you'd filter every call | O(1) get, O(m) scan per calendar |
| `list[Event]` per calendar | stored events | a calendar has tens–hundreds of events; a linear scan is simpler than an interval tree and fast enough. Flip to an interval tree past ~thousands | O(m) conflict scan |
| half-open `TimeRange` | overlap test | one boolean; alternative (closed intervals) needs special-casing touch points and breaks back-to-back booking | O(1) |
| `itertools.count` | id generation | atomic `next()` under the GIL — safe across calendars without its own lock | O(1) |

> I deliberately chose a **linear scan** over an interval tree: at calendar scale (hundreds of events, query windows of a week/month) the scan is a handful of comparisons. I'd switch to an interval/segment tree when a single calendar crosses ~5–10k events and listing becomes a measurable hot spot.

### D. Tricky Logic — claim / proof / fix

> **🧠 Claim:** Touching intervals must NOT count as a conflict.
> **🔬 Proof:** With closed intervals, booking 10–11 then 11–12 would be rejected — but those are legitimate back-to-back meetings. Users would be unable to fill a day.
> **🛠️ Fix:** `TimeRange.overlaps` uses strict `<` on a half-open `[start, end)`: `start < other.end and other.start < self.end`. `test_touching_ranges_do_not_overlap` covers it.

> **🧠 Claim:** Listing an unbounded recurrence must terminate.
> **🔬 Proof:** A `DailyRule` with no `until`/`count` ("standup forever") would loop infinitely if `expand` had no window bound.
> **🛠️ Fix:** `expand` breaks at `start > window.end` (step 6) because occurrence starts are monotonic. `test_weekly_window_clips_occurrences` proves clipping.

> **🧠 Claim:** A recurring add must be all-or-nothing.
> **🔬 Proof:** If we appended the event first and checked occurrences after, a mid-series clash would leave a half-booked series the user never confirmed.
> **🔬 Fix:** `add_recurring_event` checks *every* occurrence before the single `append`; any clash raises before mutation. `test_recurring_conflict_rejects_whole_series` proves nothing is added on failure.

> **🧠 Claim:** Monthly recurrence on day 31 must produce real dates.
> **🔬 Proof:** Naive `month += 1` yields "Feb 31", which is invalid and would crash or skip.
> **🛠️ Fix:** `MonthlyRule._nth_start` clamps the day to `monthrange(year, month)[1]`. `test_monthly_clamps_to_month_end` proves Jan-31 → Feb-28 → Mar-31.

> **🧠 Claim:** `is_free` calling `find_conflicts` must not deadlock under the lock.
> **🔬 Proof:** `is_free` takes the calendar lock, then calls `find_conflicts`, which takes the *same* lock — a plain `Lock` would self-deadlock.
> **🛠️ Fix:** the thread-safe `_lock` uses a reentrant `RLock`, so the owning thread re-enters freely. `test_no_double_booking_same_slot` exercises the locked path.

### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example

> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
> ```
> def add_event(self, calendar_id, title, time_range):
>     for e in self._calendars[calendar_id]:          # KeyError if new calendar
>         if e.time_range.start <= time_range.end and time_range.start <= e.time_range.end:
>             raise ConflictError(...)                 # closed interval: rejects back-to-back
>     self._calendars[calendar_id].append(Event(...))  # ignores recurring events entirely
> ```
> **Why it's wrong:** three bugs. (1) `<=` is a *closed* interval — it rejects legitimate back-to-back meetings. (2) It compares against each event's *first* slot only — a recurring event's later occurrences are invisible, so it double-books them. (3) `self._calendars[calendar_id]` raises `KeyError` for a new calendar. The correct version routes every event through `_expand_event` (so recurring occurrences are seen), uses half-open `overlaps`, and `setdefault`s the calendar.

**Enums + value objects (`models.py`)** — vocabulary, the overlap heart, and the carriers in one file.

```python copy
class Frequency(Enum):
    DAILY = "DAILY"
    WEEKLY = "WEEKLY"
    MONTHLY = "MONTHLY"


class EventStatus(Enum):
    CONFIRMED = "CONFIRMED"
    CANCELLED = "CANCELLED"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TimeRange:
    start: datetime
    end: datetime

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        if self.start >= self.end:
            raise InvalidTimeRangeError(
                f"start {self.start.isoformat()} must be before end {self.end.isoformat()}"
            )

    def overlaps(self, other: "TimeRange") -> bool:
        # half-open: [10,11) and [11,12) do NOT overlap
        return self.start < other.end and other.start < self.end

    @property
    def duration(self) -> timedelta:
        return self.end - self.start
```

🎙️ Say: "The overlap is one line. `__post_init__` makes a backwards range impossible — no bad span can exist anywhere."

**The Strategy seam (`strategies.py`)** — base loop + three concretes.

```python copy
class RecurrenceRule(ABC):
    frequency: Frequency

    def __init__(self, interval=1, until=None, count=None):
        if interval < 1:
            raise InvalidRecurrenceError("interval must be >= 1")
        if until is not None and count is not None:
            raise InvalidRecurrenceError("set UNTIL or COUNT, not both")
        self.interval = interval
        self.until = until
        self.count = count

    @abstractmethod
    def _nth_start(self, base_start: datetime, n: int) -> datetime:
        raise NotImplementedError

    def expand(self, event: Event, window: TimeRange) -> list[EventInstance]:
        duration = event.time_range.duration
        instances: list[EventInstance] = []
        n = 0
        while True:
            if self.count is not None and n >= self.count:
                break
            start = self._nth_start(event.time_range.start, n)
            if self.until is not None and start > self.until:
                break
            if start > window.end:
                break
            occurrence = TimeRange(start, start + duration)
            if occurrence.overlaps(window):
                instances.append(EventInstance(event.id, event.title, occurrence, event.status))
            n += 1
        return instances
```

🎙️ Say: "`expand` is the Template Method — the loop is here, the date step is the only abstract bit." ⚠️ "What if a daily rule has no end?" → "The `start > window.end` break terminates it — you only ever materialize what fits the window you asked for."

#### `add_event` — the pipeline

**1. Role:** the core booking workflow — check the slot is free, then claim it, in one step.
**2. Core logic:**
1. get-or-create the calendar's event list
2. find conflicts for the slot
3. if any → raise `ConflictError`
4. build the event with a fresh id
5. append and return

**3. Edge cases:** new calendar (must auto-create), overlapping slot (reject), a recurring event's later occurrence overlapping (caught because conflict check expands events).

**4. Code:**
```python copy
def add_event(self, calendar_id: str, title: str, time_range: TimeRange) -> Event:
    with self._lock(calendar_id):
        calendar = self._calendars.setdefault(calendar_id, [])
        conflicts = self._find_conflicts_locked(calendar, time_range)
        if conflicts:
            raise ConflictError(
                f"'{title}' overlaps {len(conflicts)} existing occurrence(s) "
                f"in calendar '{calendar_id}'"
            )
        event = Event(id=self._next_id(), title=title, time_range=time_range)
        calendar.append(event)
        return event
```

**5. Reasoning:** This is the heart of the booking path. `_find_conflicts_locked` does the real work — it expands every stored event (including recurring ones) over just this slot and returns any overlaps, so a clash against the third occurrence of a weekly meeting is caught the same as a one-off. Notice we're NOT validating `time_range` here — `TimeRange`'s constructor already guaranteed `start < end`, so by the time we hold one it's valid. The check-and-append is deliberately adjacent: in the clean version they're already one logical step, and in §9 the `_lock` makes them atomic under concurrency. The `setdefault` is what makes a brand-new calendar Just Work without a separate `create_calendar` call.

**6. 🎙️ + ⚠️:**
> 🎙️ Say: "Get the calendar, check conflicts, reject or append. The check and the append are the critical section — that's the race."
> ⚠️ "What if two people book the same free slot at once?" → "In the clean version both could pass the check. The fix is a per-calendar lock around check-and-append — only one wins, the other sees the slot taken. That's §9."

#### `add_recurring_event` — all-or-nothing

**1. Role:** book a whole repeating series atomically.
**2. Core logic:** build the event with its rule → compute a bounded check window → expand → if *any* occurrence clashes, reject the series → else append.
**3. Edge cases:** unbounded rule (use the horizon window), mid-series clash (reject everything), count/until honored by `expand`.

**4. Code:**
```python copy
def add_recurring_event(self, calendar_id, title, time_range, rule) -> Event:
    with self._lock(calendar_id):
        calendar = self._calendars.setdefault(calendar_id, [])
        event = Event(id=self._next_id(), title=title, time_range=time_range, recurrence=rule)
        check_window = self._conflict_window(time_range, rule)
        for occurrence in self._expand_event(event, check_window):
            if self._find_conflicts_locked(calendar, occurrence.time_range):
                raise ConflictError(
                    f"recurring '{title}' occurrence at "
                    f"{occurrence.time_range.start.isoformat()} overlaps an "
                    f"existing event in calendar '{calendar_id}'"
                )
        calendar.append(event)
        return event
```

**5. Reasoning:** The key decision is that the `append` happens *after* the whole loop — so a clash on occurrence #3 raises before anything is stored, and the series is never half-booked. `_conflict_window` bounds the check: a `count`- or `until`-bounded rule is naturally finite, but a truly unbounded rule ("standup forever") needs a horizon, so we check one year ahead and document that as the interview simplification. We reuse the exact same `_find_conflicts_locked` and `_expand_event` helpers as `add_event`, so single and recurring bookings can never disagree about what counts as a conflict.

**6. 🎙️ + ⚠️:**
> 🎙️ Say: "Expand the series, check every occurrence, append only if all clear — all-or-nothing."
> ⚠️ "What about a recurrence with no end?" → "I check within a one-year horizon for conflicts; the rule still expands lazily per query window. In production the horizon is configurable."

### Approach — the conflict-detection helper (`_find_conflicts_locked`)

This is the most interesting helper: *how* do you decide what a new slot clashes with, given some events repeat forever?

**Approach 1 — compare against each event's full timeline (rejected):**
```
for e in calendar:
    for every occurrence of e, ever:
        if occurrence.overlaps(slot): conflict
```
> **Challenges:** a recurring event has *infinite* occurrences — "every Monday forever" never terminates. You can't enumerate a timeline that has no end.

**Approach 2 — maintain a sorted interval tree of materialized occurrences (rejected for this scale):**
```
tree = IntervalTree()      # insert every occurrence as it's created
tree.query(slot)           # O(log n + k)
```
> **Challenges:** you'd have to materialize and store occurrences up front (back to the infinity problem) or rebuild the tree on every horizon change — state lives in two places and drifts. Worth it past ~10k events per calendar; pure overhead at interview scale.

**Chosen approach — expand each event *into the candidate slot* then linear-overlap:**
```
for e in calendar:
    for occ in expand(e, slot):     # bounded by the slot — finite, tiny
        if occ.overlaps(slot): conflict
```
> **Why it wins:** expanding *into the slot* makes even an infinite rule produce at most a couple of occurrences (whatever lands in that narrow window), so it's finite and fast. Single source of truth — the rule generates on demand, nothing is stored twice. O(m) over the calendar's events, m small.

### Helper methods

**`_expand_event`** — one expansion path for both kinds of event (and the cancelled-skip).
```python copy
def _expand_event(self, event: Event, window: TimeRange) -> list[EventInstance]:
    if event.status == EventStatus.CANCELLED:
        return []
    if event.recurrence is None:
        if event.time_range.overlaps(window):
            return [EventInstance(event.id, event.title, event.time_range, event.status)]
        return []
    return event.recurrence.expand(event, window)
```

**`_conflict_window`** — bound the check for a recurring add.
```python copy
def _conflict_window(self, first: TimeRange, rule: RecurrenceRule) -> TimeRange:
    if rule.until is not None:
        end = rule.until + first.duration
    else:
        end = first.start + timedelta(days=self._CONFLICT_HORIZON_DAYS)
    return TimeRange(first.start, max(end, first.end))
```

**`_next_id`** — atomic id generation.
```python copy
def _next_id(self) -> str:
    return f"evt-{next(self._ids)}"
```

Initial: `calendars={}`.

|---|---|---|
| 1 | `add_event("a","Standup",[09:00,09:30))` | `a=[Standup(evt-1)]` |

Back-to-back allowed, free slot reported free. ✓

**Scenario 2 — Conflict rejection:**

| Step | Call | After |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `add_event("a","A",[10:00,11:00))` | `a=[A]` |
| 2 | `add_event("a","Oops",[10:00,11:00))` | conflict found → `ConflictError`, `a` unchanged |

The invariant holds; the calendar still has exactly one event. ✓

**Scenario 3 — Race (verbal):** Without the lock, two threads call `add_event` on the same slot; both run the conflict check, both see it empty, both append — two overlapping events, invariant broken. With the per-calendar `RLock`, the first thread holds the lock through check-and-append; the second blocks, then runs its check *after* the first appended, sees the clash, and raises `ConflictError`. Exactly one wins.

| Operation | Time | Space | Key reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `add_event` | O(m) | O(1) | scan calendar's m events, expand each into the slot (tiny) |
| `add_recurring_event` | O(k·m) | O(k) | k occurrences in the check window × m events scanned each |
